---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
#library(stargazer)
library(kableExtra)
#library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-data_blue_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, inverse

.size80[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size50[
Bivariate Analyses

- Visualizations

- "Bivariate_Analyses_Notes.R"
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2024)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. ?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/00-Pizza_Politics1.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size50[**Assignment for Today:**]

.size50[
1. 'cut' and 'clarity'

2. 'cut' and 'price'

3. 'carat' and 'price' (convert 'carat' using cut)

4. 'carat' and 'price' (correlation)

.right[**Source:** 'diamonds' dataset in tidyverse]
]

???

### Did everybody complete all four of these?

<br>

### Did any of these trip you up? Is there anything we need to discuss?

<br>

(SLIDES x 5 if necessary **BUT try to blow past these and get to the new tools!**)






---

class: middle, center

.content-box-blue[.size35[**Categorical x Categorical: Frequency Tables**]]

```{r, echo=TRUE}
# Counts
table(diamonds$cut, diamonds$clarity)
```

```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
# Proportions
prop.table(table(diamonds$cut, diamonds$clarity))
```

```{r}
round(prop.table(table(diamonds$cut, diamonds$clarity)), 3)
```

???

Cut: "quality of the cut (Fair, Good, Very Good, Premium, Ideal)"

Clarity: "a measurement of how clear the diamond is (I1 (worst), SI2, SI1, VS2, VS1, VVS2, VVS1, IF (best))"

<br>

### How do we change the proportion table to compare the distributions of cut quality across each level of clarity?

### In other words, do we see evidence that jewelers are more careful when cutting an internally flawless (IF) diamond vs a diamond with "inclusions" (I1)?

- (**SLIDE**)



---

class: middle, center

.content-box-blue[.size35[**Categorical x Categorical: Frequency Tables**]]

<br>

```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
# Proportions
prop.table(table(diamonds$cut, diamonds$clarity), margin = 2)
```

.code120[
```{r}
round(prop.table(table(diamonds$cut, diamonds$clarity), margin = 2), 3)
```
]

???

(Certainly looks like it!)

<br>

### Everybody comfortable using tables to analyze the relationship between two categorical variables?

<br>

What I hope you're very quickly seeing here is that interpreting the relationships in tables is hard!

- That's why we build pictures!




---

class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size35[**Categorical x Numeric: Descriptive Statistics per Level**]]]

.pull-left[
.code90[
```{r, echo=TRUE}
# Mean
aggregate(data = diamonds, price ~ cut, 
          FUN = mean)

# Median
aggregate(data = diamonds, price ~ cut, 
          FUN = median)
```
]]

.pull-right[
.code90[
```{r, echo=TRUE}
# Range
aggregate(data = diamonds, price ~ cut, 
          FUN = range)
```
]

.code70[
```{r, echo=TRUE}
# Percentiles
aggregate(data = diamonds, price ~ cut, 
          FUN = quantile)
```
]]

???

Your next job was to analyze the relationship between the price and cut quality of the diamonds.

### What did you find?

<br>

### - Why the big differences between mean and medians?

### - What specifically can we infer based on the fact that the ranges overlap so much?
- (More factors to diamond price! Especially the size of the diamond!)

<br>

### Everybody comfortable using aggregate and descriptive statistics functions to analyze the relationship between a numeric and a categorical variable?



---

class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size35[**Numeric x Numeric: Convert One (cut)**]]]

<br>

```{r, echo=TRUE}
# Transform Numeric Variable to Categorical
diamonds$carat2 <- cut(diamonds$carat, breaks = c(0, .8, 6), 
                       labels = c("Smaller than Avg", "Larger than Avg"))
```

.center[
```{r, echo=TRUE}
# Mean price by level of new variable
aggregate(data = diamonds, price ~ carat2, FUN = mean)

# Median price by level of new variable
aggregate(data = diamonds, price ~ carat2, FUN = median)
```
]

???

Your next job was to analyze the relationship between the price and cut quality of the diamonds.

### What did you find?

- (Pretty clear that diamond size is a BIG determinant of price!)

<br>

### Everybody comfortable using cut to convert a numeric variable into a categorical variable?



---

class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size35[**Numeric x Numeric: Correlation**]]]

<br>

```{r, echo=TRUE}
# Correlation
cor(diamonds$carat, diamonds$price)

# Statistical Test
cor.test(diamonds$carat, diamonds$price)
```

???

Your next job was to analyze the relationship between the price and cut quality of the diamonds.

### What did you find?

- (A STRONG linear relationship implied by this VERY high correlation score)

<br>

### Everybody comfortable using the correlation functions on two numeric variables?

<br>

This is another approach that REALLY benefits from visualization!

- So, let's get to work visualizing bivariate relationships!




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-teal3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size60[**Bivariate Analyses**]]

.size40[
1. Descriptive Statistics by Group
    - Counts, Proportions, Means, Medians, Standard Deviations, Percentiles, Ranges, Interquartile Ranges (IQR) and Correlations

2. Visualizations
    - Bar plots (stacked, side-by-side, proportions), box plots and scatter plots
]

???

On Monday, we worked on the Descriptive Statistics by Group.

<br>

Today we dive into bivariate visualizations as a tool for analyzing a relationship.

- We'll go back to using the 'mpg' dataset built into the tidyverse





---

class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size45[**Univariate Analysis: A Categorical Variable**]]]

.pull-left[
```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='95%', fig.width=6, fig.asp=.9, cache=TRUE}
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class)) +
  geom_bar()
```
]

.pull-right[
```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='95%', fig.width=6, fig.asp=.9, cache=TRUE}

ggplot(mpg, aes(x = drv)) +
  geom_bar()
```
]

???

Variable type determines tool so let's start with two categorical variables!

<br>

Here's what we've already done: univariate analyses of individual categorical variables.

- On the left is the distribution of cars by "class"

- On the right is the distribution of drive trains

<br>

**SLIDE**: The question is what is the breakdown of drive trains in each class of cars?



---

class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size35[**Bivariate Analysis: Two Categorical Variables**]]]

```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='65%', fig.width=6, fig.asp=.6, cache=TRUE}
# Side-by-side bar plots
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class, fill = drv)) + #<<
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") #<<
```

???

*Make sure they can interpret these results*

- For bivariate case geom_bar needs an x and a fill argument inside the aes function.

<br>

By default geom_bar adjusts the width of the bars so each x label takes up the same space.

- I really don't love this default choice.

- **SLIDE**: Let's fix it.




---

class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size35[**Bivariate Analysis: Two Categorical Variables**]]]

```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='65%', fig.width=6, fig.asp=.6, cache=TRUE}
# Side-by-side bar plots
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class, fill = drv)) +
  geom_bar(position = position_dodge(preserve = "single")) #<<
```

???

### What do we learn from this? What does the relationship appear to be between class and drive train?

<br>

Now swap the 'x' and 'fill' variables in this plot.

- I want you to get a sense of what happens when you change which variable goes on the x axis and which is the fill.



---

class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size35[**Bivariate Analysis: Two Categorical Variables**]]]

.pull-left[
.code70[
```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='90%', fig.width=6, fig.asp=.75, cache=TRUE}
# Side-by-side bar plots
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class, fill = drv)) + #<<
  geom_bar(position = position_dodge(preserve = "single"))
```
]]

.pull-right[
.code70[
```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='90%', fig.width=6, fig.asp=.75, cache=TRUE}
# Side-by-side bar plots
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = drv, fill = class)) + #<<
  geom_bar(position = position_dodge(preserve = "single"))
```
]]

???

### What question does this plot on the right help us answer? How is it different from the other one?
- (What types of car class are most associated with each type of drive train)

<br>

Same data, same tool, very different questions answered!

<br>

### Questions on making a side-by-side bar plot two categorical variables?




---

class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size35[**Bivariate Analysis: Two Categorical Variables**]]]

.pull-left[
.code70[
```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='90%', fig.width=6, fig.asp=.75, cache=TRUE}
# Side-by-side bar plots
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class, fill = drv)) +
  geom_bar(position = position_dodge(preserve = "single")) #<<
```
]]

.pull-right[
.code70[
```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='90%', fig.width=6, fig.asp=.75, cache=TRUE}
# Stacked bar plots
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class, fill = drv)) +
  geom_bar(position = "stack") #<<
```
]]

.center[.size30[(See Chang Recipes 3.7, and 10 for controlling the colors and legend layout)]]

???

Here we see the second style of bivariate bar plot: A Stacked Bar Plot

### Why is the stacked bar plot better for analyzing this relationship?

- (These plots are meant to tell us how each class of car uses the various drive trains and stacked bars help you see the proportions specific to each car)

<br>

### Make sense?




<br>

### Any questions on a stacked bar plot?




---

class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size35[**Bivariate Analysis: Two Categorical Variables**]]]

.pull-left[
.code70[
```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='90%', fig.width=6, fig.asp=.75, cache=TRUE}
# Stacked bar plots
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class, fill = drv)) +
  geom_bar(position = "stack") #<<
```
]]

.pull-right[
.code70[
```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='90%', fig.width=6, fig.asp=.75, cache=TRUE}
# Proportional bar plots
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class, fill = drv)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") #<<
```
]]

???

Last style of common bar plot is a proportional stacked bar plot.

- Super cool approach transforms each bar to a proportion of 100% per x variable level.

- So, the y-axis marks off the proportion of the total represented by each level of the fill variable.

<br>

Clearly makes comparisons and summary easier, BUT...

### Why are these super dangerous?

- Look on the left plot, very FEW 2seaters and minivans so the proportions are much less stable

- Only takes adding one of this class with a different drive train to dramatically change the %'s

<br>

### Any questions on our three versions of the bar plot?

- Remember, need to specify both an 'x' and a 'fill' variable PLUS a 'position' argument in the geom_bar



---

class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size35[**Bivariate Analysis: Categorical x Numeric Variable**]]]

.pull-left[
.code70[
```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='90%', fig.width=6, fig.asp=.75, cache=TRUE}
# Box Plot (Horizontal)
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = drv, y = cty)) +
  geom_boxplot()
```
]]

.pull-right[
.code70[
```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='90%', fig.width=6, fig.asp=.75, cache=TRUE}
# Box Plot (Vertical)
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = cty, y = drv)) +
  geom_boxplot()
```
]]

???

Here is where we see the real utility of box plots.

- For comparing the distribution of numeric variables across the levels of a categorical variable.

<br>

### Remind me, what are the main components of a box plot?
- (Median is the black line)
- (IQR is the box)
- (Whiskers extend 1.5x the IQR)
- (Outliers identified)

<br>

### And what do we learn from these boxes about this relationship?



---

class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size35[**Bivariate Analysis: Numeric x Numeric Variable**]]]

```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='65%', fig.width=7, fig.asp=.65, cache=TRUE}
# Scatter Plot
ggplot(mpg, aes(x = displ, y = cty)) +
  geom_point()
```

???

Our final new tool: Scatter Plots

### Any questions on how to read this plot?

<br>

### What do we learn from this?

<br>

### What statistic could we calculate to help us explain the relationship?
- (Correlation!)

Go for it!

```{r}
cor.test(mpg$displ, mpg$cty)
```



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-teal3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size60[**Bivariate Analyses**]]

.size40[
1. Descriptive Statistics by Group
    - Counts, Proportions, Means, Medians, Standard Deviations, Percentiles, Ranges, Interquartile Ranges (IQR) and Correlations

2. Visualizations
    - **Bar plots (stacked, side-by-side, proportions), box plots and scatter plots**
]

???

### Any questions on the tools we learned today?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size50[**For next class analyze the relationship between:**]

.size50[
1. 'cut' and 'clarity' (x3: side-by-side bars, stacked bars, AND proportion bars)

2. 'cut' and 'price' (box plots)

3. 'carat' and 'price' (scatter plot)

.right[**Source:** 'diamonds' dataset in tidyverse]
]

???



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size50[**For Today:**]

.size50[
1. 'cut' and 'clarity' (x3: side-by-side bars, stacked bars, AND proportion bars)

2. 'cut' and 'price' (box plots)

3. 'carat' and 'price' (scatter plot)

.right[**Source:** 'diamonds' dataset in tidyverse]
]

???

### How did this go?



---

class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size35[**Diamonds: 'cut' and 'clarity'**]]]

.pull-left[
.code60[
```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='90%', fig.width=6, fig.asp=.65, cache=TRUE}
# Side-by-side bar plots
ggplot(diamonds, aes(x = cut, fill = clarity)) +
  geom_bar(position = position_dodge(preserve = "single")) +
  labs(x = "Quality of the Cut", y = "", fill = "Clarity") +
  theme_bw()
```
]]

.pull-right[
.code60[
```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='90%', fig.width=6, fig.asp=.65, cache=TRUE}
# Proportional stacked bar plots
ggplot(diamonds, aes(x = cut, fill = clarity)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(x = "Quality of the Cut", y = "", fill = "Clarity") +
  theme_bw()
```
]]

???



---

class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size30[**Diamonds: 'cut' and 'price'**]]]

```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='60%', fig.width=9, fig.asp=.65, cache=TRUE}
ggplot(diamonds, aes(x = price, y = cut)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "seagreen2") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "Prices", y = "Quality of the Cut") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::dollar_format())
```



---

class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size30[**Diamonds: 'carat' and 'price'**]]]

```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, out.width='60%', fig.width=9, fig.asp=.65, cache=TRUE}
ggplot(diamonds, aes(x = carat, y = price)) +
  geom_point(alpha = .1) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "Size of the Diamond (carats)", y = "Prices") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar_format())
```
